perturbation.original_text: Venue for any action to enforce the provisions of this Agreement shall be properly laid in any state or federal court in the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: Venue for any action to enforce the provisions of this Agreement shall be properly laid exclusively in the courts of Delaware, regardless of the parties' principal places of business or the location where the cause of action arises.
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that all actions must be brought in Delaware, even if the cause of action arises in Minnesota or involves parties primarily located there, the contract attempts to override the established principles of personal jurisdiction and venue under federal law, potentially depriving parties of their right to sue in a forum where jurisdiction is proper and convenient.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391 because it attempts to restrict venue to a single state (Delaware) regardless of whether that venue is proper under federal venue statutes. Section 1391(b) states that venue is proper in a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a judicial district where any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located. The modified clause eliminates these options, potentially requiring parties to litigate in a venue that lacks a significant connection to the dispute.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391, as it restricts venue to Delaware regardless of where the cause of action arises or the parties' principal places of business. This conflicts with the general venue provisions outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which allow for venue in districts where a substantial part of the events occurred or where any defendant resides. The original text allowed venue in Minnesota, which is consistent with potential locations of the cause of action or parties. The changed text's exclusive venue clause in Delaware overrides these federal venue statutes, potentially forcing parties to litigate in a forum with no connection to the dispute, therefore creating a direct contradiction. The scraped law snippet supports this by highlighting that 28 USC 1391 governs venue generally, and the modified clause attempts to circumvent this.

perturbation.original_text: BBY agrees that for a period of fifteen (15) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will directly or indirectly solicit to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"); provided, however, that in the absence of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period, general recruitment advertising or communications to which such Restricted Employee responds shall not be considered a solicitation for purposes of this Section 9.
perturbation.changed_text: BBY agrees that for a period of fifteen (15) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will directly or indirectly solicit to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"). This restriction includes responses to general recruitment advertising or communications, regardless of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the carve-out for general recruitment advertising, the non-solicitation clause becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable under California law, which favors employee mobility and restricts overly broad non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification likely violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes enforceable, overly broad restrictions that prevent responding to general job postings can be viewed as an unlawful restraint on trade under California law, as it restricts employees' ability to seek better opportunities.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the carve-out for general recruitment advertising, broadening the non-solicitation agreement. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as explained by the LLM, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in 16600.(a) that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void."

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that either party or any of its Representatives have breached this Agreement, then the breaching party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the reasonable legal fees incurred in connection with such litigation, including an appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall not be entitled to recover attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome of the litigation or any statutory rights to attorney's fees.
perturbation.explanation: This provision attempts to waive the right to attorney's fees, even if such fees are mandated by statute (e.g., in cases of wage disputes).
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Labor Code Section 218.5, which allows a court to award reasonable attorney's fees and costs to the prevailing party in any action brought for the nonpayment of wages. The modified text attempts to waive this statutory right, which may be unenforceable as against public policy.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 218.5. (a) In any action brought for the nonpayment of wages, fringe benefits, or health and welfare or pension fund contributions, the court shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party to the action requests attorney’s fees and costs upon the initiation of the action. However, if the prevailing party in the court action is not an employee, attorney’s fees and costs shall be awarded pursuant to this section only if the court finds that the employee brought the court action in bad faith. This section shall not apply to an action brought by the Labor Commissioner. This section shall not apply to a surety issuing a bond pursuant to Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 7000) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code or to an action to enforce a mechanics lien brought under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 8400) of Title 2 of Part 6 of Division 4 of the Civil
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Labor Code Section 218.5. The original text allows for the recovery of attorney's fees by the non-breaching party. The changed text explicitly states that the prevailing party shall *not* be entitled to recover attorney's fees, regardless of statutory rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: "In any action brought for the nonpayment of wages... the court shall award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party if any party to the action requests attorney’s fees and costs upon the initiation of the action." The changed text attempts to waive a statutory right, creating a legal inconsistency and contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of Minnesota, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the Principality of Monaco, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof, and all disputes will be settled by private arbitration in Monaco.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying the laws of Monaco and mandating private arbitration there, the agreement could deprive employees of rights and protections afforded to them under US labor laws (if the employees are working in the US), such as those related to discrimination, wage and hour, and workplace safety. This could be especially problematic if the Monaco laws offer significantly weaker protections or remedies than U.S. law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2) if the agreement is applied to employees working in the United States. Title VII prohibits discrimination in employment based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. By requiring disputes to be settled under the laws of Monaco, a jurisdiction with potentially different or weaker anti-discrimination laws, the agreement could effectively waive an employee's right to pursue a Title VII claim in U.S. courts or under U.S. law. Even with an arbitration clause, U.S. law generally requires that arbitration provide equivalent substantive and procedural rights as would be available in court, which may not be the case under Monaco law or in a Monaco-based arbitration.

Home statutes Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 EDITOR'S NOTE: The following is the text of Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (Title VII) (Pub. L. 88-352), as amended. Title VII is codified at 42 U.S.C. 2000e and in subsequent sections. Title VII amendments include those introduced by the Civil Rights Act of 1991 (CRA) and the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009 . Editor’s notes appear in italics and include cross-references to the codified version of Title VII. Title VII prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex and national origin. An Act To enforce the constitutional right to vote, to confer jurisdiction upon the district courts of the United States to provide injunctive relief against discrimination in public accommodations, to authorize the attorney General to institute suits to protect constitutional rights in public facilities and public education, to extend the Commission on Civil Rights, to prevent discrimination in federally assisted programs, to establish a Commission on Equal Employment Opportunity, and for other purposes. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That this Act may be cited as the "Civil Rights Act of 1964". * * * DEFINITIONS SEC. 2000e. [Section 701] For the purposes of this subchapter- (a) The term "person" includes one or more individuals, governments, governmental agencies, political subdivisions, labor unions, partnerships, associations, corporations, legal representatives, mutual companies, joint-­stock companies, trusts, unincorporated organizations, trustees, trustees in cases under Title 11 [originally, bankruptcy ], or receivers. (b) The term "employer" means a person engaged in an industry affecting commerce who has fifteen or more employees for each working day in each of twenty or more calendar weeks in the current or preceding calendar year, and any agent of such a person, but such term does not include (1) the United States, a corporation wholly owned by the Government of the United States, an Indian tribe, or any department or agency of the District of Columbia subject by statute to procedures of the competitive s
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 42 - THE PUBLIC HEALTH AND WELFARE 42 U.S.C. United States Code, 2010 Edition Title 42 - THE PUBLIC HEALTH AND WELFARE CHAPTER 21 - CIVIL RIGHTS SUBCHAPTER VI - EQUAL EMPLOYMENT OPPORTUNITIES Sec. 2000e-2 - Unlawful employment practices From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §2000e–2. Unlawful employment practices (a) Employer practices It shall be an unlawful employment practice for an employer— (1) to fail or refuse to hire or to discharge any individual, or otherwise to discriminate against any individual with respect to his compensation, terms, conditions, or privileges of employment, because of such individual's race, color, religion, sex, or national origin; or (2) to limit, segregate, or classify his employees or applicants for employment in any way which would deprive or tend to deprive any individual of employment opportunities or otherwise adversely affect his status as an employee, because of such individual's race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. (b) Employment agency practices It shall be an unlawful employment practice for an employment agency to fail or refuse to refer for employment, or otherwise to discriminate against, any individual because of his race, color, religion, sex, or national origin, or to classify or refer for employment any individual on the basis of his race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. (c) Labor organization practices It shall be an unlawful employment practice for a labor organization— (1) to exclude or to expel from its membership, or otherwise to discriminate against, any individual because of his race, color, religion, sex, or national origin; (2) to limit, segregate, or classify its membership or applicants for membership, or to classify or fail or refuse to refer for employment any individual, in any way which would deprive or tend to deprive any individual of employment opportunities, or would limit such employment opportunities or otherwise adversely affect his status as an employee or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by specifying that the laws of Monaco govern the agreement and mandating private arbitration in Monaco. This potentially deprives employees working in the U.S. of protections under U.S. labor laws, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. By requiring disputes to be settled under Monaco law, which may have weaker anti-discrimination laws, the agreement could effectively waive an employee's right to pursue a Title VII claim in U.S. courts, thus contradicting U.S. law. Specifically, 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a) states that it is unlawful for an employer to discriminate against an individual based on protected characteristics. Therefore, the change undermines the legal protection provided by Title VII.
